figures.caption: Fig. 5: Zero-shot demonstration rebirth with RoboDream. We retrieve existing trajectories from the DROID dataset (Right) and rebirth them in our target setting. Given new object priors and scene priors (Left), the model synthesizes photorealistic demonstrations (Middle) where robot interacts with specified objects in the specified scene, adhering to the retrieved motion.

Fig. 6: Compositional generation with RoboDream. We demonstrate our claim that actions, objects, and scenes are distinct, recombinable elements. Starting from a single base trajectory, we can interpret it in multiple ways by changing the inputs: (1) Novel Instances: Manipulating new instances (e.g., different markers) by changing the object prior. (2) Novel Scenes: Placing the robot in a new environment by changing the scene prior. (3) Novel Tasks: Changing the task context (e.g., marker-to-bowl vs cube-to-cup) by swapping object priors. (4) Novel Viewpoints: Generating demonstrations from a new camera angle by re-rendering the motion and capturing a corresponding scene prior. All generations shown are zero-shot, using objects and scenes unseen during training.

Fig. 2: The RoboDream architecture. The model generates a video o1:T conditioned on multiple inputs: a rendered robot-only video vrob (kinematic anchor), a scene prior Is (background), and an object prior Io (visual appearance). We concatenate the latent video features with the encoded robot motion and scene prior, while injecting the object prior tokens via extended self-attention. Additionally, the task instruction and global robot trajectory are injected via cross-attention to ensure semantic and kinematic consistency.
Fig. 4: Real-world evaluation setup using the DROID robot platform. We evaluate on four tasks: Put Marker into Bowl, Remove Marker from Bowl, Put Cube into Cup, and Wipe Table with Towel.
